Tip 2 - Shoot during golden hour, always

This is the single most impactful habit you can build. Plan your most important
Blue hour- the 20 minutes after sunset- is equally valuable for cityscapes and l
Tip 3 - Look for leading lines
Leading lines are roads, paths, fences, rivers, staircases, or any linear elemen
When you arrive at a new location, scan the scene for natural lines before raisi
Tip 4 - Change your perspective
The eye-level, standing-straight shot is the default for most people- and it is
Get down to ground level for street markets, children, animals, or flowers. Find
Tip 5 - Include people for scale and story
Architecture and landscape photos gain immediate impact when a human figure is i
You do not need to ask strangers to pose. A distant figure walking away from cam
Changing your perspective transforms familiar scenes into striking compositions
Tip 6 - Pack only the gear you will actually carry
The best camera is the one you have with you. This is not just a cliche- it is t
For most travel situations, a practical kit looks like this:
- Body: A lightweight mirrorless or your smartphone
- Lens: A 24-70mm equivalent covers most travel situations
- Extra batteries: At least two- charging infrastructure is unpredictable
- Storage: Multiple small cards over one large card- a failed card should not ru
- ND filter: Useful for waterfalls and bright midday light
Leave the tripod unless your primary goal is long-exposure or astrophotography.
Tip 7 - Shoot RAW for editing flexibility
RAW files preserve all the data captured by the sensor- they are unprocessed and
With a RAW file, recovering a slightly overexposed sky or lifting shadow detail

Tip 8 - Enable GPS tagging and use it deliberately
Every photo your smartphone takes can embed GPS coordinates in the EXIF metadata
On iPhone, go to Settings -> Privacy & Security -> Location Services -> Camera a
With GPS-tagged photos, you can:
- Visualize your entire trip on an interactive map with SammaPix TravelMap
- Automatically sort photos by country or city using SammaPix GeoSort
- Recall exactly where a specific photo was taken years later
- Return to the same spot for a second attempt or a seasonal comparison
For DSLR and mirrorless cameras that lack built-in GPS, most manufacturers offer
One privacy note: GPS data travels with the image file when you share it. Before

Building a complete travel photography workflow
These 10 tips work best as a connected system rather than individual tricks. Her
- Before the trip: Research locations, identify golden hour times, plan your pri
- Gear check: Enable GPS tagging, charge batteries, format cards
- In the field: Apply composition principles consciously, vary perspective, prio
- End of day: Back up cards, do a rough cull on your laptop
- After the trip: Final cull, edit selected photos, organize by location using S
- Before sharing: Strip GPS from photos you share publicly using the EXIF Remove
